speaker: interviewer
text: Tape number two, survivor is Abraham Bomba. The date is August 14, 1996. Mr. Bomba, we left off talking about when did life begin to change for you. Let me just ask you one question here. At this point that we're talking about, were you working?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of work were you doing?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm a barber, and I worked in barber shop.

speaker: interviewer
text: We started to talk about changes. What year are we talking about now?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, now, the year 1939. We changed altogether.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: What had happened all of sudden it was on a Friday, September 1st, 1939, where the army of Germany crossed the border of Poland, and they went into Poland. And the first city they went in was the city I was in it, Czestochowa, because this is on the border of the German country.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were-- what did you personally observe that day? Where were you and what were you doing?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we-- that was in the morning we start running around to get-- to get, I think, and to run somewhere farther away from the German border. But before we started anything, the German were already in the city. We couldn't go nowhere. And it's still on the afternoon of that day, we left this city of Czestochowa and we went outside about [INAUDIBLE], about 40 kilometers.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who's we? Who?

speaker: interviewee
text: That means me, my mother, my little brother. My older brother got married. He was with wife. He had a child. His wife was also pregnant at that time, the nine-- in the nine month. And they didn't go. So we left. We went to a place called Radomsko, which is not far away from Czestochowa. We got over there, and they start bombing the place. And before we looked around, the German were all over. And we were far away from home. So we decided to go back where we have a still a little apartment and our belonging and to stay home, because it's no use to run away where they reaching us before even we got there. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We went back home. That was Friday. And Saturday was a little bit quiet. And Sunday after dark, they started coming more and more, not only from the German army, also from the party, like the black SS. They wore black uniforms and red uniforms with the arm bands with the swastika. Monday morning, they started to take out all the Jews from the part of the Jewish quarters, which wasn't exactly the Jewish quarter, because over there, also, non-Jews lived over there too. But they took all of them out. They took them to various places, like a place where they had the Polish army left over there. Myself and thousands of other people, they took into a church, a very big church.

speaker: interviewer
text: With your mother and brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Where they where, my brother was a different place. My mother was a different place. I was there myself. And over there, they started going into the church, putting the ammunition, killing people. Huge number of people are killed going in to the church, in the church.

speaker: interviewer
text: They were shooting?

speaker: interviewee
text: Shooting. And over the-- over the-- a couple of days. And all of a sudden, out-- they let us out from the church. And we went back home.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you with men and women, or just men in the church?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hm?

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you with men and women in the church?

speaker: interviewee
text: Men.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just men.

speaker: interviewee
text: Just men, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did they lock you inside the--

speaker: interviewee
text: They-- they didn't have to lock us inside because at the front of the door, there was SS. So there was no use to-- to lock us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they--

speaker: interviewee
text: Nobody-- nobody anyway could escape.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they give you any food at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. They didn't give us any food. But we had over there priest. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And he did for us a lot, a lot. He said, don't be ashamed. Use any place for a toilet. You get water, whatever we can do, we will help you. His name was Frubleski.

speaker: interviewer
text: Father Frubleski?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. He was very, very, very fine man from the priest. In Catholics, he was one of the finest which I know of.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he was inside the church with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was inside with us, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was he actually arrested and put in there, or was he--

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: You don't know.

speaker: interviewee
text: No I wouldn't know. I don't know. Then we went home. And we started to build up a little bit of our-- our life. I went back to work. And everything was like little bit normal.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your mother was back home--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --and your brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother was back home, and my brother was back home. My little brother was also back. And we started to live by day by day. And the life was very, very hard because money we didn't have. And the prices started to rise, like a bread. We paid for a bread $0.50 or $1 for 2 pound. It grew to about $3, $4. It was very hard, that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there were any other restrictions placed upon your life at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, later on, yes. Later on, should I say, you know, few weeks later was an order. Jewish people can go out up till-- excuse me-- up to 7 o'clock. You got to be inside between 7:00 and 7:00 in the morning. They couldn't go out. Any-- anyway, any place, they couldn't leave the houses, not even emergency. All that was, oh, little by little was going on like that. And then come deference orders. Deference orders means like taking out Jewish people from better apartments and giving away to Volksdeutsche, which Volksdeutsche means people who lived in Poland, and their great-great-grandparents used to be Germans. They called them Volksdeutsche. All they took the apartment for themself. Or they give those good apartments for Polish people, which they work together with the Nazis. And it was like every day was worst and worst because they cordoned off the Jewish people. It wasn't a ghetto yet. [INAUDIBLE] so a high number of Jewish people, which they came in the Germans. You got to leave, take in five minutes whatever you can, and out. So they have to leave everything and just to go.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where could they go? Some of them, they had some relatives in the poor quarters. And some, they went into just to public places, like [GERMAN], which supported the poor people. And that-- that was very, very hard.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then they started taking away clothes, taking away fur, especially. Fur is not allowed ever at all for Jewish people. Wherever they caught somebody wearing a fur coat or even small things, they killed them, because was an order to give that up. They needed that for the army.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have to wear anything identifying yourself as a Jew at this point?

speaker: interviewee
text: That-- that started in the end of 1939. I think around December was an order to wear blue and white arm bands with a Star of David.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you attach it to your clothing?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the front. No, no. Excuse me. On the arm, on the left arm.

speaker: interviewer
text: You had to sew it on, or could you remove it?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, you could remove it, because sewing on was too much, because people wore different clothes. So we just took off the armband and put on-- make those with a little [INAUDIBLE]. And it was also an order whoever will not wear that thing and will go out will be shot. And they did it. So that was the order at that time. Until [INAUDIBLE] they start Jewish press, there was no paper. To know anything was not allowed. To gather this-- to gather, let's say, more than four or five people at one time, it was not allowed, because they are called a meeting. So was not allowed to even to talk to each other about what had happened. But that was going on until about '41. And '41, they created the big ghetto in Czestochowa.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, where was this ghetto in relation to where you were living?

speaker: interviewee
text: The ghetto was in the poor place, in a poor neighborhood. They opened up, let's say, about six, seven streets, which was for the Jewish people. And in so many days, two or three days, they have to move out from that apartment and to move into the Jewish quarters.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you had to leave your apartment?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I lived in that place.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did any--

speaker: interviewee
text: I lived in that poor neighborhood, so we stayed in. But it happened also that the people that came from the better neighborhood, which they have to leave their places, we had to take in. Somebody had a bigger apartment, they were assigned two, three families got to take in. So naturally, it was very crowded.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many people were with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, with us was about seven people in one room.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you sleep?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, we slept.

speaker: interviewer
text: You had beds or--

speaker: interviewee
text: We slept on the floor, bed, but everybody slept. We slept. And it was-- it was a very hard time at time. Some of them, you know, they brought in with them some money. Some of them, they did not. And we had to share whatever we could. And I would say at the time of sorrow, like that, what we had, the people were very generous helping each other. There were not mean, like it happened sometime. So did the best to-- to survive.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- what are we-- what year are we talking about now that the ghetto was established?

speaker: interviewee
text: '41. It was--

speaker: interviewer
text: What time of the year.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was-- yes. To be exactly, I think, if I'm not mistaken, it was in May, 1941.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this ghetto surrounded by any gate, barbed wire, fence?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who guarded it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Guarded-- guarded by the Jewish police, Polish police, Ukrainian, and Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: The people inside the ghetto, were they mainly from Czestochowa or were they brought in from other areas?

speaker: interviewee
text: The beginning was mainly from Czestochowa. But after that, people came from smaller places around Czestochowa. And we had to take them in. It growed-- it grew the population from about 35 when the war started until over 50,000 when the ghetto was established.

speaker: interviewer
text: When the ghetto was established, it started at 35,000 people?

speaker: interviewee
text: When it started. And when the ghetto was populated, the people around in the smaller places, we had over 50,000 Jewish people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there a Judenrat? You mentioned there was that Jewish police.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yeah, there was a Judenrat. Of course there was a Judenrat.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know anything about it? Who was on it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Chosen by the Gestapo. It happened always that he was people very well known.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know any of them personally?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I know-- I know, I would say, often.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who-- who was it in the Judenrat?

speaker: interviewee
text: President, who was by the name of [? Kopinsky, ?] which were a very rich man, and a few more of them, a few doctors, a few more of them. And I would say they're very, very nice people. They could what they-- they did what they could.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was--

speaker: interviewee
text: But they couldn't do much.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was their actual job, the Judenrat?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, the actually job was support the Germans, whatever the need. They ordered them, let's say, contribution $100,000 so that they have to guard that. They order them they need, let's say, a couple of thousand people to work. They did Judenrat had to get those people.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the food situation like inside the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, the food situation was very bad. We were starving almost, in the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you given food? Was there any kind of rationing system? How did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a rationing, from-- from the also from Judenrat. We got little coupons. But we couldn't do much with it.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you eat?

speaker: interviewee
text: Whatever we could.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was it? What-- what could you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Some bread, some potatoes. Meat, we didn't see.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you work inside the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Was a barber.

speaker: interviewer
text: Whose hair were you cutting?

speaker: interviewee
text: Jewish, and people, also. Oh, I had a sign, let's say, from the Judenrat, a couple hundred people. They were coming in with their coupons. I had to cut their hair, free for nothing. That was-- I would think that we were not taking out from the city and sent some places to get killed. There was a-- there was a good job for us, because we were on the place.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever leave the inside of the ghetto to go out yourself?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know of anybody who tried to leave and escape.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Some of them, they escaped. And some of them, it happened they killed them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you witness anybody being mistreated inside the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Many times, by the Germans, many times.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you see?

speaker: interviewee
text: And by the Ukraines, also.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you see?

speaker: interviewee
text: Beating, cutting of the beard, cutting off the [INAUDIBLE], taking off the clothes. Many, many of it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Had they done anything that they-- that you know of, that-- and that's why these treatments were handed out to them?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, because they're Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there a particular German officer who was in charge of the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: His name was [? Dagenhardt. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: Dagen--

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Dagenhardt, ?] he was the head man from the Gestapo. And he was like the king of the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any personal interaction--

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: --with him?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know of any type of organized or unorganized resistance within the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: At that time, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: At that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: At that time, no. We had later on.

speaker: interviewer
text: But when you were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have friends or any kind of social life at all inside the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very little. Very little, beside-- beside reading. And also, it was-- it was not allowed to have books, which they had some kind of political underground, like from socialist or Zionist, or other thing. So the first thing was we got rid of the books. I, myself, I must have, I would say, at least a couple thousand of books.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you had to get rid of the books?

speaker: interviewee
text: You had to rid.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a girlfriend?

speaker: interviewee
text: A girlfriend? Yes, I had.

speaker: interviewer
text: Inside the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you meet her before you went into the ghetto?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. Were you married? I married that girlfriend.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before you went into the ghetto or in--

speaker: interviewee
text: In the ghetto, when the ghetto started.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was her name.

speaker: interviewee
text: Her name was Reizl.

speaker: interviewer
text: And her maiden name?

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Prepalitski. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: And you married-- you had a marriage inside the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: I married her inside the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who married you?

speaker: interviewee
text: A rabbi.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any-- you had a ceremony?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we had a ceremony. We had a small wedding, just the family.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you able to have any kind of celebration after the wedding?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we had no celebration. Where-- where could we celebrate? We couldn't celebrate.

speaker: interviewer
text: So then did your wife move in with your-- you and your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I took-- I took a room. for a lady that she had an empty place for her children [INAUDIBLE]. And I moved in. But we stayed over there not too long. I would say about five, six months. And then it started. They-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] they take out the Jewish people and to send them away.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there different aktions that started occurring?

speaker: interviewee
text: We-- yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did the first one, start would you say?

speaker: interviewee
text: The first one started on September the 22nd, 1942.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, what I remember of that, the first thing was that all of a sudden, all the starting from 1939 up to '42, there were no lights at night in the streets. And all of a sudden, not just that night, just before September the 22nd, all the lights were on. And police and Gestapo and all kind from them surrounded the ghetto. And they called the lowest streets, one, two, out from the houses. And I was between that too. They took us out at that time. Me, at that time was already my wife, and I had a small baby from four weeks.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the baby's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My baby's name was after mine father, Will. And they took out 6-- over 6000 Jewish people, man, woman, young, old. And we went to the railroad station. It happened that coming to the railroad station, all the train cars, they're full. They couldn't push in anymore. And they order us to go back to our home. The 6,000 they left, you went home.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned you had a four-week-old baby. The baby was born inside the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there a hospital?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was a hospital.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of medical treatment was given?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I just mentioned before even in a-- in an emergency, nobody could go out from the house. My wife, she was at that-- she was pregnant. She-- she's supposed to give birth, but she couldn't go out to the hospital until 7 o'clock in the morning. And she was suffering the whole night until after 7:00. I was allowed to go out and to take her to the hospital. And that--

speaker: interviewer
text: The hospital was inside the ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: Inside, yes. It was a small-- it wasn't the big Jewish hospital what we had in-- in our-- in our town. And over there, she had the baby. She had a big boy, very big baby for that, you know? For people they had nothing to eat, it was at that time big baby, about seven pound.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there able to be a circumcision for the baby at all.

speaker: interviewee
text: Hm, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: After the baby was born?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes, were circumcision to the-- to the eight days, the way he was supposed to, yes. We did it. It was done, also, without the knowledge of the German authority.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what happened when you were sent back from the train.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we were sent back from the railroad station. People came over in [INAUDIBLE]. Oh, that what it is. Now, you are released. Now, you are supposed to be happy because they needed 6,000 people, and they got them. And they would not take any more. Yes? Fine. We gotten back to our little apartment, stayed there for couple of days. But we thought some day, they-- it's the same thing, the Ukrainian and the Jewish police and the Polish and the Germans, they were still surrounded around. And that happened three days later on a Friday, the first day of [? Sohkot. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: Another aktion?

speaker: interviewee
text: Another. At that time we were from the same street. They took us out, and we were sent to the marketplace. At the marketplace was a selection. If you know what a selection is--

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, selection is to select people to go to work or to go to debt. And mostly, what they-- mostly what they did is they're separated.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who's they? Who did the selections?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Germans. For the Germans, which on top of them was this one [? Dagenhardt. ?] I mentioned it before. They separated people. What that means is separate them. Like they left a husband. They took away the wife. Left the wife, they took away the-- the husband. Or they took away everybody. In my thing, when I came to the place where this selection was held, they didn't select nobody. Everybody to the railroad station.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: So-- they needed more people. So I went that time with my wife, my little brother. My big brother with his wife and kid went already with the first aktion.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother went with me.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your baby?

speaker: interviewee
text: And my wife and my baby. And also was--

speaker: interviewer
text: Also who?

speaker: interviewee
text: My-- also was my wife's sister with her husband, also pregnant lady. She was in nine month. For eight years she couldn't have a baby. They just at that time. So went her the same. And then I had some relatives also going with me the same one. And we came into the railroad station. They put them in the-- in the railroad train. They locked us. There was no train of people to ride, but they're just like for animals, I think.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many people were in the car with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Between-- in every car was between, I would say, 140 to 160 people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you sit down?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Hardly. We just stayed, one-- one on top of the other. And there was some job of it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there any food?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no food, no water.

speaker: interviewer
text: We have to stop this tape now. We will continue with the next tape shortly.
